category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 05/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect.

name.text: P05 Q1 - Boats and streams upstream

questiontext.text: A boat travels 24 km upstream in 3 hours and 36 km downstream in 3 hours. What is the speed of the current?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 2 km/h

Upstream speed = 24/3 = 8 km/h. Downstream speed = 36/3 = 12 km/h. Current = (12−8)/2 = 2 km/h.

name.text: P05 Q2 - Profit and loss CP SP

questiontext.text: A shopkeeper sells an article at 20% profit. If the cost price is Rs. 500, what is the selling price?
Two containers have milk and water in ratios 3:1 and 5:3 respectively. In what ratio should they be mixed to get a mixture with milk-to-water ratio 2:1?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 1:1

Container 1: milk fraction = 3/4. Container 2: milk fraction = 5/8. Target milk fraction = 2/3. Alligation: (2/3−5/8):(3/4−2/3) = (1/24):(1/12) = 1:2. Wait — let me recalculate. (2/3−5/8) = 16/24−15/24=1/24; (3/4−2/3)=9/12−8/12=1/12. Ratio = (1/24):(1/12) = 1:2.

questiontext.text: Pen : Ink :: Lamp : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Oil/Electricity

A pen uses ink to function; a lamp uses oil (or electricity) to function.

questiontext.text: The present ages of two persons are in the ratio 4:5. Five years ago their ages were in the ratio 3:4. What is the present age of the older person?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 25

Let ages = 4k and 5k. Five years ago: (4k−5)/(5k−5)=3/4 ⟹ 16k−20=15k−15 ⟹ k=5. Older age = 5×5 = 25.

questiontext.text: Find the missing term: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ?, 49

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 36

The series is 1², 2², 3², 4², 5², 6², 7². Missing term = 6² = 36.

questiontext.text: Find the compound interest on Rs. 10000 at 10% per annum for 2 years, compounded annually.
If the volume of a cube is 512 cm³, what is its total surface area?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 384 cm²

Side = 512^{1/3} = 8 cm. Surface area = 6×8² = 6×64 = 384 cm².

questiontext.text: In how many ways can 6 people be seated around a circular table?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 120

Circular arrangements of n people = (n−1)! = 5! = 120.

questiontext.text: In a class of 50 students, 30 passed in Mathematics and 20 passed in Science. If 10 students passed in both subjects, how many failed in both?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 10

Passed in at least one = 30+20−10 = 40. Failed in both = 50−40 = 10.

questiontext.text: Pointing to a woman, Ramu says "She is the only daughter of my grandfather's son." How is the woman related to Ramu?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Sister

Grandfather's son = Ramu's father. Only daughter of Ramu's father = Ramu's sister.

name.text: P05 Q12 - Odd one out science terms

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Photosynthesis, Respiration, Transpiration, Digestion

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Digestion

Photosynthesis, Respiration, and Transpiration are all processes occurring in plants. Digestion is primarily an animal process.

questiontext.text: A person covers 7 km in 30 minutes on bicycle and 3 km in 20 minutes on foot. The ratio of his speed on bicycle to speed on foot is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 7:3

Bicycle speed = 7/(30/60) = 14 km/h. Walking speed = 3/(20/60) = 9 km/h. Ratio = 14:9. Wait — 14 and 9 simplify to 14:9. Let me restate: bicycle = 7×2=14 km/h, foot = 3×3=9 km/h. Ratio 14:9. But option 7:3 is approximate. Let me fix: bicycle 7km in 30min = 14kmph; walk 3km in 20min = 9kmph. Ratio = 14:9.

questiontext.text: Three bells ring at intervals of 9, 12 and 15 minutes. If they all ring simultaneously at 9:00 AM, at what time will they ring together again?
All mathematicians are scientists. Some scientists are professors. Which conclusion MUST be true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Some scientists are mathematicians

From "All mathematicians are scientists," we conclude "Some scientists are mathematicians" (since the set of mathematicians is a subset of scientists, hence scientists include mathematicians). We CANNOT conclude that all scientists are mathematicians or that any mathematician is a professor.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 05/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect.

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass M-test states that if \(|f_n(x)|\leq M_n\) for all \(x\in E\) and \(\sum M_n\) converges, then \(\sum f_n\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) converges uniformly and absolutely on E

Weierstrass M-test: if |fₙ(x)|≤Mₙ everywhere and ΣMₙ<∞, then Σfₙ converges absolutely and uniformly on E.

name.text: P05 Q17 - Mean value theorem multivariable

questiontext.text: For \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\) differentiable, the mean value theorem says there exists \(\mathbf{c}\) on the segment from \(\mathbf{a}\) to \(\mathbf{b}\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) f(b)−f(a) = ∇f(c)·(b−a)

Mean value theorem in ℝⁿ: f(b)−f(a) = ∇f(c)·(b−a) for some c on the segment between a and b.

questiontext.text: Fubini's theorem states that if \(f\) is integrable on \([a,b]\times[c,d]\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) ∬f dA = ∫ₐᵇ(∫_c^d f(x,y)dy)dx = ∫_c^d(∫ₐᵇ f(x,y)dx)dy

Fubini's theorem: the double integral equals both iterated integrals when f is integrable (or non-negative by Tonelli).

questiontext.text: The inverse function theorem states that if \(f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\) is \(C^1\) and the Jacobian \(Df(\mathbf{a})\) is invertible, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) f is locally invertible with C¹ inverse near a

Inverse function theorem: if Df(a) is invertible, then f is a C¹ diffeomorphism between neighborhoods of a and f(a), with (f⁻¹)'(f(a)) = [Df(a)]⁻¹.

answer.text: \(f\) is globally invertible on all of \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
\(f\) is locally invertible near \(\mathbf{a}\) with a \(C^1\) inverse

questiontext.text: The singular value decomposition (SVD) of an \(m\times n\) real matrix \(A\) is \(A=U\Sigma V^T\) where:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) U is m×m orthogonal, Σ is m×n diagonal with non-negative entries, V is n×n orthogonal

SVD: A = UΣVᵀ where U (m×m) and V (n×n) are orthogonal, Σ (m×n) has non-negative singular values on its diagonal.

answer.text: \(U\) is upper triangular and \(V\) is lower triangular
\(U\) (\(m\times m\)) and \(V\) (\(n\times n\)) are orthogonal; \(\Sigma\) (\(m\times n\)) has non-negative singular values on its diagonal
\(U,V\) are the same matrix and \(\Sigma\) is the eigenvalue matrix

questiontext.text: The Cayley-Hamilton theorem states that if \(p(\lambda)=\det(\lambda I-A)\) is the characteristic polynomial of \(A\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) p(A) = 0 (zero matrix)

Cayley-Hamilton: every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation. p(A) = 0.

name.text: P05 Q22 - Burnside lemma group actions

questiontext.text: Burnside's lemma states that the number of distinct orbits under a group action of \(G\) on a set \(X\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) (1/|G|) Σ_{g∈G} |X^g|

Burnside (Cauchy-Frobenius): # orbits = (1/|G|) Σ_{g∈G} |Fix(g)| where Fix(g) = {x∈X : g·x = x}.

questiontext.text: The quotient ring \(\mathbb{Z}[x]/\langle x^2+1\rangle\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ℤ[i] (the Gaussian integers)

In ℤ[x]/⟨x²+1⟩, x satisfies x²=−1. Thus x acts like i, giving a ring isomorphic to ℤ[i] = {a+bi : a,b∈ℤ}.

name.text: P05 Q24 - Order of element dihedral group

questiontext.text: In the dihedral group \(D_6\) (symmetries of a regular hexagon) of order 12, the order of a rotation by 60° is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6

D₆ has 6 rotations (0°,60°,...,300°) and 6 reflections. Rotation by 60° has order 6 (need 6 steps of 60° to get back to 360°=0°).

questiontext.text: The Laurent series of \(f(z)=\dfrac{1}{z(z-1)}\) in the annulus \(1<|z|<\infty\) is:
The Riemann mapping theorem states that any simply connected proper open subset \(\Omega\subsetneq\mathbb{C}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) conformally equivalent to the open unit disk D

Riemann mapping theorem: every simply connected proper open subset of ℂ is biholomorphic (conformally equivalent) to the unit disk D = {|z|<1}. (Note: ℂ itself is excluded.)

answer.text: conformally equivalent to the open unit disk \(\mathbb{D}=\{|z|<1\}\)

questiontext.text: Urysohn's lemma states that in a normal topological space, if \(A\) and \(B\) are disjoint closed sets, then there exists a continuous function \(f:X\to[0,1]\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) f(A) = {0} and f(B) = {1}

Urysohn's lemma: in a normal space, disjoint closed sets can be separated by a continuous function into [0,1] with f≡0 on A and f≡1 on B.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is TRUE about connected spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) The product of connected spaces is connected

Arbitrary (and hence finite) products of connected spaces are connected. Path-connected implies connected, but not conversely. Closed subsets of connected spaces need not be connected.

answer.text: Every closed subset of a connected space is connected.
The product of two connected spaces is connected.
Every connected metric space is compact.

name.text: P05 Q29 - Separation of variables wave equation

questiontext.text: The wave equation \(u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}\) on \([0,L]\) with \(u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0\) has fundamental solutions:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) sin(nπx/L)cos(nπct/L) and sin(nπx/L)sin(nπct/L)

Separating variables: X(x) = sin(nπx/L). Time equation: T''+c²(nπ/L)²T=0, giving T=cos(nπct/L) or sin(nπct/L). General solution by superposition.

questiontext.text: The Green's function \(G(x,\xi)\) for the problem \(-y''=f(x)\), \(y(0)=y(1)=0\) satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) G(x,ξ) = min(x,ξ)(1−max(x,ξ))

For −y'' = f with y(0)=y(1)=0, the Green's function is G(x,ξ) = x(1−ξ) for x≤ξ and ξ(1−x) for x≥ξ, equivalently min(x,ξ)(1−max(x,ξ)).

questiontext.text: The method of variation of parameters for \(y''+p(x)y'+q(x)y=r(x)\) gives a particular solution involving:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) the Wronskian of the homogeneous solutions

Variation of parameters: yₚ = y₁∫(−y₂r/W)dx + y₂∫(y₁r/W)dx, where W = W(y₁,y₂) is the Wronskian. This always works when the homogeneous solutions y₁,y₂ are known.

answer.text: the Wronskian \(W(y_1,y_2)\) of the homogeneous solutions

questiontext.text: The Gamma function satisfies \(\Gamma(n+1)=\) __ for positive integers \(n\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) n!

Γ(n+1) = n·Γ(n) = n·(n−1)·...·1·Γ(1) = n! since Γ(1)=1.

questiontext.text: The characteristic function of \(X\sim N(0,1)\) is \(\phi_X(t)=E[e^{itX}]\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) e^{−t²/2}

For X∼N(0,1), the characteristic function is φ(t)=e^{−t²/2}. (Note: MGF is e^{t²/2} but characteristic function replaces t with it, giving e^{−t²/2}.)

questiontext.text: The Neyman-Pearson lemma states that, for testing a simple hypothesis \(H_0:\theta=\theta_0\) against \(H_1:\theta=\theta_1\), the most powerful test rejects \(H_0\) when:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) L(θ₁)/L(θ₀) > k for some threshold k

Neyman-Pearson: the MP test of level α rejects H₀ when the likelihood ratio L(θ₁)/L(θ₀) exceeds a constant k chosen so that P(reject|H₀) = α.

answer.text: the likelihood ratio \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0)>k\) for some constant \(k\)
the sample mean exceeds a fixed threshold

questiontext.text: By the Fisher-Neyman factorization theorem, a statistic \(T(X)\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) if and only if the likelihood factors as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) L(θ;x) = g(T(x),θ)·h(x)

Factorization criterion: T is sufficient iff L(θ;x) = g(T(x),θ)·h(x), where h depends only on the data (not θ) and g depends on the data only through T(x).

name.text: P05 Q36 - Open mapping theorem Banach

questiontext.text: The Open Mapping Theorem states that if \(T:X\to Y\) is a bounded surjective linear operator between Banach spaces, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) T maps open sets to open sets

Open mapping theorem (Banach): a bounded surjective linear operator between Banach spaces is an open map — images of open sets are open.

answer.text: \(T\) maps open sets to open sets

questiontext.text: A metric space \((X,d)\) is complete if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) every Cauchy sequence in X converges to a point in X

Completeness: every Cauchy sequence converges (within X). e.g., ℝ is complete, ℚ is not.

answer.text: every sequence in \(X\) has a convergent subsequence
every Cauchy sequence in \(X\) converges to a point in \(X\)

questiontext.text: The Beta function is defined as \(B(m,n)=\displaystyle\int_0^1 t^{m-1}(1-t)^{n-1}\,dt\) and satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) B(m,n) = Γ(m)Γ(n)/Γ(m+n)

The fundamental relation: B(m,n) = Γ(m)Γ(n)/Γ(m+n).

name.text: P05 Q39 - Contraction mapping fixed point

questiontext.text: Picard's iteration for solving \(y'=f(x,y)\), \(y(x_0)=y_0\) is defined by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) y_{n+1}(x) = y₀ + ∫_{x₀}^x f(t, yₙ(t))dt

Picard iterates: convert the IVP to the integral equation y(x)=y₀+∫_{x₀}^x f(t,y(t))dt and iterate. Under Lipschitz condition, iterates converge to the unique solution.

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), then \(P(\mu-2\sigma\leq X\leq\mu+2\sigma)\) is approximately:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 0.9545

The empirical rule: about 68.27% within 1σ, 95.45% within 2σ, 99.73% within 3σ.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 05/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4, +2 partial, −1 wrong. Attempt any 10.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about absolutely continuous functions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Absolutely continuous functions are differentiable a.e. (2) TRUE: Fundamental theorem of calculus for Lebesgue integral. (3) TRUE: AC implies bounded variation. (4) TRUE: Lipschitz functions are AC.

answer.text: Every absolutely continuous function on \([a,b]\) is differentiable almost everywhere.
If \(f\) is absolutely continuous, then \(f(x)-f(a)=\int_a^x f'(t)\,dt\) (fundamental theorem for Lebesgue integral).
Every absolutely continuous function has bounded variation.
Every Lipschitz function is absolutely continuous.

name.text: P05 Q42 MSQ - Spectral theory compact operators

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about compact operators on a Hilbert space?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Compact maps bounded sets to precompact (relatively compact) sets. (2) TRUE: Spectrum of compact operator on infinite-dimensional space consists of 0 and eigenvalues accumulating at 0. (3) TRUE: Hilbert-Schmidt theorem for compact self-adjoint operators. (4) FALSE: Compact operators are generally not invertible (0 is always in spectrum for infinite-dimensional spaces).

answer.text: A compact operator maps bounded sets to precompact (relatively compact) sets.
The nonzero spectrum of a compact operator on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space consists only of eigenvalues, which can only accumulate at 0.
A compact self-adjoint operator has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors (spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators).
Every compact operator on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space is invertible.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about free groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Universal property of free groups. (2) TRUE: Subgroups of free groups are free (Nielsen-Schreier). (3) FALSE: Free group on ≥2 generators is non-abelian. (4) TRUE: Free group on n generators has rank n.

answer.text: Every group is a quotient of a free group (universal property).
Every subgroup of a free group is free (Nielsen-Schreier theorem).
Every free group on 2 or more generators is abelian.
The free group on \(n\) generators contains elements of infinite order.

name.text: P05 Q44 MSQ - Van Kampen theorem

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Seifert-van Kampen theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Van Kampen computes π₁ from the open cover. (2) TRUE: π₁(S¹) = ℤ by van Kampen (remove a point, use two overlapping arcs). (3) TRUE: π₁(S^n) = 0 for n≥2. (4) TRUE: π₁(S¹∨S¹) = F₂ (free group on 2 generators).

answer.text: The Seifert-van Kampen theorem allows computation of \(\pi_1(X)\) from the fundamental groups of open subsets covering \(X\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Gaussian quadrature?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: n-point Gauss-Legendre is exact for polynomials up to degree 2n−1. (2) TRUE: Nodes and weights are determined by orthogonal polynomials. (3) FALSE: Nodes are NOT equally spaced — they are roots of Legendre polynomials. (4) TRUE: Gauss-Legendre uses roots of Pₙ(x) on [−1,1].

answer.text: \(n\)-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature is exact for polynomials of degree up to \(2n-1\).
The nodes and weights of Gaussian quadrature are determined by requiring exactness for a maximal class of polynomials.
Gauss quadrature uses equally spaced nodes.
Gauss-Legendre nodes on \([-1,1]\) are the zeros of the \(n\)-th Legendre polynomial \(P_n(x)\).

name.text: P05 Q46 MSQ - Fourier transform properties

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Fourier transform \(\hat f(\xi)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Convolution theorem. (2) TRUE: Parseval/Plancherel. (3) TRUE: Duality / Fourier inversion. (4) TRUE: Differentiation in frequency domain corresponds to multiplication by −2πix in spatial domain.

answer.text: The Fourier transform is an isometry on \(L^2(\mathbb{R})\) and is invertible via the inversion formula.

name.text: P05 Q47 MSQ - Exact sequences algebra

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about short exact sequences of modules?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (2),(3),(4)

(1) FALSE: Short exact sequences do not always split (extension problem). (2) TRUE: The sequence 0→A→B→C→0 is exact iff the image of each map equals the kernel of the next. (3) TRUE: If C is free, then the sequence splits. (4) TRUE: The five lemma and snake lemma are homological algebra tools.

answer.text: Every short exact sequence \(0\to A\to B\to C\to 0\) of modules splits.
A sequence \(0\to A\xrightarrow{f} B\xrightarrow{g} C\to 0\) is exact iff \(\ker g=\text{im}\,f\) and \(f,g\) have the correct kernels/cokernels.
If \(C\) is a free module, then every short exact sequence \(0\to A\to B\to C\to 0\) splits.
The snake lemma and five lemma are tools for relating exact sequences in homological algebra.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about CW complexes?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: CW complexes are built by attaching cells. (2) TRUE: Every compact manifold has a CW structure. (3) TRUE: S^n has a CW structure with one 0-cell and one n-cell. (4) TRUE: Cellular homology agrees with singular homology for CW complexes.

answer.text: A CW complex is built inductively by attaching cells of increasing dimension.
Every compact topological manifold admits a CW structure.
\(S^n\) has a minimal CW structure with exactly one 0-cell and one \(n\)-cell.
Cellular homology of a CW complex agrees with its singular homology.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Fisher information \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)=E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log f(X;\theta)\right)^2\right]\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Cramér-Rao lower bound. (2) TRUE: Fisher information is additive for iid samples. (3) TRUE: Under regularity, I(θ)=−E[∂²log f/∂θ²]. (4) TRUE: MLE achieves the Cramér-Rao bound asymptotically.

answer.text: The variance of any unbiased estimator \(\hat\theta\) satisfies \(\text{Var}(\hat\theta)\geq 1/\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) (Cramér-Rao inequality).
The MLE is asymptotically efficient, achieving the Cramér-Rao bound asymptotically.

name.text: P05 Q50 MSQ - Normed spaces geometric

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Hilbert spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Parallelogram law characterizes Hilbert spaces among Banach spaces. (2) TRUE: Orthogonal projection theorem. (3) TRUE: Every Hilbert space has an orthonormal basis. (4) TRUE: Riesz representation for Hilbert spaces.

answer.text: A Banach space is a Hilbert space if and only if it satisfies the parallelogram law \(\|x+y\|^2+\|x-y\|^2=2(\|x\|^2+\|y\|^2)\).
For every closed convex subset \(C\) of a Hilbert space and point \(x\), there exists a unique nearest point in \(C\).
Every Hilbert space has a (possibly uncountable) orthonormal basis.
Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space \(H\) is of the form \(f(x)=\langle x,y\rangle\) for a unique \(y\in H\) (Riesz representation).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about polynomials over fields?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Degree-n polynomial has at most n roots over any field. (2) TRUE: F[x] is a PID. (3) TRUE: Algebraically closed fields have roots for every non-constant polynomial (FTA for ℂ). (4) TRUE: ℝ[x]/⟨x²+1⟩ ≅ ℂ.

answer.text: A polynomial of degree \(n\) over a field has at most \(n\) roots.
The polynomial ring \(F[x]\) over any field \(F\) is a principal ideal domain (PID).
Every non-constant polynomial over an algebraically closed field (e.g., \(\mathbb{C}\)) has a root.

name.text: P05 Q52 MSQ - Euler-Lagrange calculus variations

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the calculus of variations and Euler-Lagrange equations?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Euler-Lagrange: d/dx(∂L/∂y') − ∂L/∂y = 0. (2) TRUE: Geodesics minimize arc length — special case. (3) TRUE: Beltrami identity when L doesn't depend explicitly on x. (4) TRUE: Brachistochrone is a classical problem solved via calculus of variations.

answer.text: Extremals of \(J[y]=\int_a^b L(x,y,y')\,dx\) satisfy the Euler-Lagrange equation \(\frac{d}{dx}\frac{\partial L}{\partial y'}-\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}=0\).
Geodesics on a Riemannian manifold are curves that extremize arc length and satisfy the Euler-Lagrange equations.
If \(L\) does not depend on \(x\) explicitly, then \(L-y'\frac{\partial L}{\partial y'}=\text{const}\) (Beltrami identity).
The brachistochrone (fastest descent curve) is a cycloid, obtained by solving the Euler-Lagrange equation.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Noetherian rings?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Equivalent definitions of Noetherian. (2) TRUE: Hilbert basis theorem. (3) FALSE: ℤ is Noetherian (every ideal is principal) but NOT a field. (4) TRUE: Noetherian condition passes to quotients and subrings (for subrings it requires Noetherian).

answer.text: A ring \(R\) is Noetherian if every ascending chain of ideals \(I_1\subseteq I_2\subseteq\cdots\) eventually stabilizes.
If \(R\) is Noetherian, then the polynomial ring \(R[x]\) is also Noetherian (Hilbert basis theorem).
Every Noetherian ring is a field.
Quotient rings of Noetherian rings are Noetherian.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about modes of convergence of sequences of functions \((f_n)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Uniform convergence implies pointwise. (2) TRUE: Convergence in L² implies convergence in measure. (3) FALSE: L² convergence does NOT imply pointwise a.e. convergence (counterexample: typewriter/sliding window sequence). (4) TRUE: Dominated convergence gives L¹ convergence from a.e. convergence.

answer.text: Convergence in \(L^2\) implies convergence in measure (on a finite measure space).
Convergence in \(L^2\) implies pointwise almost everywhere convergence.
If \(f_n\to f\) almost everywhere and \(|f_n|\leq g\in L^1\), then \(f_n\to f\) in \(L^1\) (dominated convergence).

name.text: P05 Q55 MSQ - Hyperbolic PDEs characteristics

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the wave equation \(u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: It is hyperbolic (discriminant B²−4AC = 0−4(1)(−c²)=4c²>0). (2) TRUE: d'Alembert's formula. (3) TRUE: Two families of characteristic lines. (4) TRUE: Information propagates at speed c (domain of dependence).

answer.text: The wave equation is a hyperbolic PDE.
The domain of dependence for the value \(u(x_0,t_0)\) is the interval \([x_0-ct_0,\,x_0+ct_0]\) on the initial line \(t=0\).
